Plots of Functions of Several Variables                                                                                             The plane ax + by + cz = d
                                                                                                                                                                z
    I In Engineering and Science, we often need to deal with
      complicated systems that depend on many variables. For
      example, the ideal gas equation relates the pressure P of                                                                                                 d
      a gas in a container as a function of the temperature T of                                                                                                c

      the gas and the volume V of the container:
                                 T
                 P (T, V ) = k     ,     (k is a constant).                                                                                                                d
                                 V                                                                                                                              0          b
                                                                                                                                                                                                                      y
    I Recall that the graph of a function of one variable
                                                                                                                                                  d
      y = f (x) is a curve in two-dimensional space R2 . The                                                                                      a
      graph of a function of two variables z = f (x, y) is a
      surface in three-dimensional space R3 .                                                                                          x
    I The co-ordinate axes x, y, z form a right-handed system.

Summary                                                                                                                     Partial Differentiation
   I The co-ordinate axes x, y, z form a right-handed system.                                                                   I If y = f (x) where f : R → R, then y is the dependent
                                                                                                                                  variable, and x is the independent variable. The
   I ax + by + cz = d is a plane.                                                                                                 derivative, dy/dx or f ′ (x), is the rate of change of y with
                                                                                                                                  respect to x.
   I (x − x0 )2 + (y − y0 )2 + (z − z0 )2 = a2 is a sphere centred                                                              I If z = f (x, y) where f : R2 → R, then z is the dependent
     at (x0 , y0 , z0 ) with radius a.                                                                                            variable, and x and y are the independent variables.
   I z = c(x2 + y 2 ) is a paraboloid of revolution.                                                                            I If we treat y as a constant and differentiate with respect
          p                                                                                                                       to x we obtain ∂f /∂x, the partial derivative of f with
   I z = c x2 + y 2 is a cone with semi-vertical                                                                                  respect to x. This number is the rate of change of z in
     angle tan−1 (1/c).                                                                                                           the x direction (holding y fixed).
                                                                                                                                I If we treat x as a constant and differentiate with respect
   I If a variable is missing in the equation, then the function                                                                  to y we obtain ∂f /∂y, the partial derivative of f with
     is independent of this variable. Extend the two                                                                              respect to y. This number is the rate of change of z in
     dimensional curve to infinity in this direction.                                                                             the y direction (holding x fixed).

  Example 13                                                                                                                  The Chain Rule(s)
  Let z = f (x, y) = x2 sin y + x3 y + y 5 . Find
                                                                                                                                 I A common situation is that z is a function of x and y,
                2          2           2                      2
               ∂ z        ∂ z        ∂ z               ∂ z                                                                         with x and y themselves functions of other variables,
                   ,           ,         ,                 .
               ∂x2        ∂y 2      ∂x∂y              ∂y∂x                                                                         say u and v. Then z is also a function of u and v, and it
                                                                                                                                                                                ∂z
                                                                                                                                   makes sense to ask the question “What is        ?”
                                                                                                                                                                                ∂u
                                                                                                                                 I The chain rule enables us to answer this question without
                                                                                                                                   actually ever having to calculate z as an explicit function
                                                                                                                                   of u and v. It is usually simpler to use the chain rule than
                                                                                                                                   to differentiate directly the compositive function

                                                                                                                                                    w(u, v) := z(x(u, v), y(u, v)).

                                                                                                                                 I Three versions of the chain rule are shown below.
                                                                                                                                   However, all you need to remember is that you just apply
                                                                                                                                   the single-variable chain rule to each argument of z.
                                                  .   .   .     . . . .     . . . .     . . . .       .   .       .   .   .                                                     .   .   .     . . . .     . . . .     . . . .       .   .       .   .   .

 I It can be shown that f (x) ≈ Pn (x) when x is close to a.
   The higher the degree n, the better the approximation.
 I The infinite sum obtained by sending n → ∞ is called the
   Taylor series of f (x) about a.
